A failed rebuild now broadcasts to the browser overlay (as the architecture docs promise), and reverting a file to its last-good content after a failed build correctly triggers the recovery rebuild instead of being hash-skipped. Assorted doc corrections: the overlay WebSocket path (`/__pyxle__/overlay`), the scaffold's generated `.env.local` in the project-structure tree, a verbatim `pyxle routes` sample, a legacy-Tailwind-v3 note in the styling guide, and MiB units in the security guide.

- **Fix: React UI libraries that resolve to a CommonJS build no longer crash SSR.** A dependency without an `exports` map (e.g. `lucide-react`, common across the shadcn/ui ecosystem) resolved to its CommonJS build during the server render, whose `require('react')` failed with `Dynamic require of "react" is not supported` ŌĆö a 500 on any page importing it. The SSR bundler now resolves dependencies **ESM-first** (prefers the `module`/ESM entry over CommonJS `main`, as Vite's SSR does), so those packages link cleanly against the runtime's React. A genuinely CommonJS-only package that still `require`s a runtime module now fails with an actionable error naming the module and the page, pointing at an ES-module version or `<ClientOnly>`, instead of the bare esbuild message. See [Third-party packages ŌåÆ CommonJS packages and SSR](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/third-party-packages.md#commonjs-packages-and-ssr).

- **Docs: the road to a fully-typed Pyxle.** A new [roadmap page](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/fully-typed-pyxle.md) lays out where Pyxle's type story is heading ŌĆö authoring your client in TypeScript and forwarding Python types across the boundary so your component's `data` is typed from the Python that produced it ŌĆö grounded in what already works today (`pyxle check`, `pyxle typecheck`, Pydantic `@action` validation). JavaScript remains fully first-class; nothing here changes today's behavior.
- **Breaking prerequisite: Node.js 20.19+ is now required.** Node 18 is end-of-life; Vite 7 (below) needs Node `>=20.19`. Update your Node before upgrading. The scaffold now declares `"engines": { "node": ">=20.19" }`, and [Installation](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/installation.md) states the new floor.
- **Modernized scaffold ŌĆö React 19, Vite 7, and an interactive `pyxle init`.** A fresh project now ships **React 19** and **Vite 7** (with the Vite-7-compatible `@vitejs/plugin-react` 5), and `pyxle init` is **interactive**: polished arrow-key prompts (highlighted defaults, Enter to accept) ask whether you want Tailwind CSS, whether you want shadcn/ui (only when Tailwind is on), and whether to customize the default `@/*` import alias ŌĆö the value input appears only if you opt in ŌĆö then scaffold only what you chose. Non-interactive callers (CI, scripts, non-TTY stdin) never block ŌĆö pass `--yes` for defaults, or the explicit `--tailwind/--no-tailwind`, `--shadcn/--no-shadcn`, and `--import-alias` flags. `pyxle init .` (or `pyxle init` in an empty directory) scaffolds into the current directory and derives the project name from it. Fresh installs pass `npm audit` clean. See [Quick Start](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/quick-start.md).
- **Tailwind CSS is now opt-in ŌĆö and, when chosen, Tailwind v4 wired straight into Vite.** Previously every project got Tailwind v3 through a side-channel (`tailwind.config.cjs`, `postcss.config.cjs`, a pre-compiled `public/styles/tailwind.css`, and a standalone `tailwindcss --watch` process). That whole mechanism is gone. Choose Tailwind at `pyxle init` and you get **Tailwind v4** via the `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin and a single `@import "tailwindcss";` CSS entry ŌĆö no config files, no separate watcher, no output-CSS-in-`public/`, and therefore no "Tailwind writes into a watched dir" rebuild loop. Pyxle adds the plugin to the Vite config it generates only when the project depends on `@tailwindcss/vite`. Decline Tailwind and you get a clean baseline where **plain CSS and CSS Modules** work out of the box (both verified in dev and build). CSS Module class names are generated deterministically, so the server-rendered markup and the client bundle match exactly ŌĆö no React 19 hydration mismatch. See [Styling](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/styling.md).
- **shadcn/ui support.** Choose shadcn at `pyxle init` (it implies Tailwind) and the scaffold pre-configures `components.json`, the `@` import alias, `lib/utils.js`, and a Tailwind v4 stylesheet with the shadcn theme ŌĆö so `npx shadcn@latest add button` works with no `shadcn init` step. Every project also gets a root `vite.config.js` (re-exporting Pyxle's generated config) and a `jsconfig.json`, so the Vite ecosystem and editors find a config and resolve the import alias. Pyxle resolves that alias in both the Vite client build **and** the SSR runtime, so aliased imports render on the server too. See [Third-party packages ŌåÆ shadcn/ui](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/third-party-packages.md#shadcnui).
- **Fix: streaming SSR no longer serializes across requests.** Concurrent requests to a page that streams (`<Suspense>` / `loading.pyxl`) used to render strictly one at a time: each connection's first byte arrived only after the *previous* stream had fully finished, so several visitors hitting the same slow page saw a time-to-first-byte staircase (roughly 0.5s, 1s, 3s, 6sŌĆ”) even though each render was mostly idle waiting on its own boundaries. An SSR worker now handles many renders **concurrently** ŌĆö overlapping requests all begin rendering immediately and interleave, so every visitor gets the shell in tens of milliseconds regardless of how many others are mid-render. Each render's per-request state (pathname, CSRF token, `<Head>` tags, inline styles) is isolated with `AsyncLocalStorage`, so interleaving can never render one visitor's token or head tags into another's page. As part of this, `pyxle serve` now **auto-sizes** its SSR worker pool by default (previously a single process), so production renders in parallel out of the box; `pyxle dev` still runs a single worker. In-worker render concurrency defaults to 16 and is tunable with `PYXLE_SSR_WORKER_CONCURRENCY`. See the [Streaming guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md#concurrent-streams-dont-queue), [Deployment ŌåÆ SSR workers](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/deployment.md#ssr-workers), and [Environment variables](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/environment-variables.md#ssr-rendering).
- **`pyxle init` no longer scaffolds a stale framework pin.** The generated `requirements.txt` used to hard-code `pyxle-framework>=0.4.1,<0.5`, so `pyxle install` in a fresh project silently *downgraded* the framework that scaffolded it. The requirement is now rendered from the running version ŌĆö running `0.7.0` emits `pyxle-framework>=0.7.0,<0.8` (current release up to the next minor).
- **`pyxle typecheck` now fails fast with a clear error when TypeScript isn't installed.** It previously fell back to `npx --yes tsc`, which resolves npm's placeholder `tsc` package ŌĆö relaying its banner and ending with a self-contradictory "Type check failed with 0 error(s)". The command now checks for a real `tsc` (local `node_modules/.bin`, then global) *before* building and exits with `TypeScript is required for 'pyxle typecheck' ŌĆö run: npm install --save-dev typescript` (or "run: npm install" when it's declared but not installed). A failed `tsc` run that produces no diagnostics now reports the exit code instead of "0 error(s)". See [TypeScript guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/typescript.md).
- **Scaffolded `AGENTS.md` corrected on auto-injected names.** It claimed `LoaderError` had to be imported from `pyxle.runtime` ŌĆö false since 0.6.1, and contradicted by the rest of the file. It now consistently documents that `server`, `action`, `LoaderError`, `ActionError`, `ValidationActionError`, and `invalidate_routes` are all auto-injected by the compiler.
- **`pyxle check` works out of the box ŌĆö `pyxle-langkit` is now a default dependency.** On a fresh install, `pyxle check` ŌĆö quick-start step 6 ŌĆö exited 1 on a brand-new scaffold with "JSX checker unavailable" because the JSX layer lived behind the undocumented `[langkit]` extra. The extra is no longer needed: `pip install pyxle-framework` now pulls in `pyxle-langkit`, so the full check (Python syntax, semantics, and JSX) passes on generated files with zero extra steps. The `[langkit]` extra remains for backward compatibility but is now redundant. See [Installation](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/installation.md) and [Quick Start](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/quick-start.md).
- **Fix: the CSRF cookie no longer collides between Pyxle apps on the same host.** The cookie was literally `pyxle-csrf` with `Path=/`, and browsers scope cookies to a host ŌĆö ignoring the port ŌĆö so two Pyxle dev servers on `127.0.0.1` kept overwriting each other's token and every action in the other app failed with `403 CSRF token mismatch`. The default cookie name is now namespaced by the app's bind port (`pyxle-csrf-8000`), a stable per-app discriminator in dev *and* behind a production reverse proxy, and the document shell injects the effective name into every page (`window.__PYXLE_CSRF_COOKIE__`) so `<Form>`/`useAction` always read the right cookie. Pin an explicit name with the existing `csrf.cookieName` key. **Upgrade note:** a leftover `pyxle-csrf` cookie from an earlier version is simply ignored ŌĆö the token re-issues under the namespaced name on the next page load; no action needed. See [Security ŌåÆ CSRF protection](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security.md#csrf-protection) and [Configuration ŌåÆ CSRF](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#csrf).
- **Fix: multipart forms (file uploads) now pass CSRF with a `_csrf_token` field.** Posting an action as `multipart/form-data` with a correct `_csrf_token` field was rejected with `403 CSRF token missing` ŌĆö the middleware only read urlencoded bodies and the `X-CSRF-Token` header, so a progressive-enhancement `<Form>` containing a file input was broken before hydration. The middleware now stream-parses a multipart body just far enough to obtain `_csrf_token` (which `<Form>` renders before all other fields) and then replays the consumed bytes downstream untouched ŌĆö file parts are never buffered by the CSRF layer, so uploads of any size work. The pre-token scan is capped at 1 MiB; past the cap (or when the field is absent) the request is rejected with a `403` that says exactly what to do: send the token in the `X-CSRF-Token` header, or place `_csrf_token` before the file fields. `missing` vs `mismatch` errors are now accurate on every path, including a new `CSRF cookie missing` message when a token is submitted without its cookie half. See [Security ŌåÆ Form bodies and uploads](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security.md#form-bodies-and-uploads).
- **`pyxle dev` output is clean and readable by default, with `--verbose` for the firehose.** The dev console used to dump Vite's raw line-by-line stdout and a wall of internal build chatter, burying the one thing you need ŌĆö the URL. `pyxle dev` now prints a curated startup summary (the local URL, the Vite URL, the route count, and the total "ready in X ms"), a concise one-line notice per incremental rebuild (`Rebuilt ŌĆ” in X ms`), and any warnings or errors ŌĆö nothing else. The raw `[vite] ŌĆ”` firehose and the step-by-step internals are hidden behind `--verbose` (or the global `pyxle -v dev`), which restores the full output for troubleshooting. Genuine signal ŌĆö errors, warnings, the URLs, and rebuild success/failure ŌĆö is always shown. `pyxle serve` (production) output is unchanged. See [CLI ŌåÆ pyxle dev](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/cli.md#pyxle-dev).
- **Server-side logs now stream to the browser console during `pyxle dev`.** Your server-side `logging` output (from loaders, actions, and your own modules) is forwarded over the dev overlay's WebSocket to the browser devtools console, prefixed `[pyxle:server]` and mapped to the matching `console` method (`info`ŌåÆ`console.info`, `warning`ŌåÆ`console.warn`, `error`ŌåÆ`console.error`) ŌĆö so you can follow server logs without leaving the browser. By default only `INFO`+ from your own loggers is forwarded; `--verbose` also forwards `DEBUG` records and the framework's internal loggers. The bridge is bounded and non-blocking (it never stalls the event loop, drops records when no tab is connected or a send fails, guards against re-entrancy, and throttles bursts), and is strictly development-only ŌĆö it never runs under `pyxle serve` and never ships in the production bundle. See [CLI ŌåÆ pyxle dev](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/cli.md#pyxle-dev) and [Architecture ŌåÆ the error overlay](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/dev-server.md#the-error-overlay).
- **`public/` changes are picked up on refresh instead of triggering a rebuild (matching Next.js).** Editing, adding, or deleting a file under `public/` no longer runs a build pass or forces a browser reload. Static assets are served live from disk, so an edited file shows up on the next request/refresh, and dev now serves public assets with a revalidating `no-cache` header (previously a one-hour cache masked edits until it expired). The dev server keeps a lightweight watch on `public/` for the sole purpose of keeping its static-file index current, so a **newly added or removed** asset becomes (un)discoverable without restarting `pyxle dev`. This also removes the root cause of the Tailwind reload loop: with `tailwind.config.*` and no PostCSS config, the standalone `tailwindcss --watch` compiler writes `public/styles/tailwind.css` into the watched tree ŌĆö that write used to trigger a rebuild that let Tailwind re-emit the CSS, a self-sustaining loop (about once a second, most visible on Linux). Because `public/` writes no longer rebuild, the loop cannot occur; genuine CSS edits still hot-reload through the `pages/styles/*.css` source path. The rebuild watch (`pages/` and any `dev.watch` extras) still ignores generated build output ŌĆö `.pyxle-build`/`__pycache__` trees, `*.pyc` bytecode, and `*.db`/`*.db-wal`/`*.db-shm` journals. See [Styling ŌåÆ Legacy Tailwind v3 projects](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/styling.md#legacy-tailwind-v3-projects) and [Architecture ŌåÆ the watcher](https://pyxle.dev/docs/architecture/dev-server.md#the-watcher).
- **Watch extra directories for hot reload with `dev.watch`, and ignore extra paths with `dev.ignore`.** Pyxle watches `pages/` for hot reload, but a shared Python module imported from outside it (e.g. `lib/` or `components/`) would not trigger a rebuild. A new `dev` config block closes that gap: `dev.watch` lists extra project-relative directories to also watch (a change under one runs the normal rebuild + module reload), and `dev.ignore` lists extra glob patterns to skip. `dev.ignore` is **additive** to the built-in generated-output ignores ŌĆö those are load-bearing and always stay in force, so a user list can only add to them, never clear them. `dev.watch` entries must resolve inside the project root; a traversal path (`../ŌĆ”`) or absolute path is rejected at config load. Both default empty, and both are dev-only (ignored by `pyxle serve`). See [Configuration ŌåÆ Development](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#development).

### Documentation
- **The flagship WebSockets example no longer crashes SSR when copy-pasted.** The first example in the [WebSockets guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/websockets.md) passed `window.location.pathname` to `useWebSocket()` ŌĆö evaluated during the server render, where `window` doesn't exist, so following the guide verbatim 500'd the page. The example now derives the socket path from the route's loader data (the loader returns the `room` segment; the component templates it into the same path the page was served from), and a callout explains the rule: the hook never *connects* during SSR, but its arguments are evaluated there ŌĆö build paths from props/params, not browser globals.
- **Fixed a broken `@action` example in the [pyxle-db docs](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-db.md).** The request-scoped-access example inserted an undefined `title` instead of reading the request body; it now uses `body = await request.json()` (`request.json` is an async method ŌĆö subscripting it without awaiting fails at runtime), matching the [Server Actions](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/server-actions.md) page.
- **Documented CSRF on the pyxle-auth credential endpoints.** `POST /auth/login` / `/auth/signup` / `/auth/logout` are CSRF-protected like every state-changing POST, so a raw curl per the old docs got `403` with no explanation. A new [CSRF subsection](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-auth.md#csrf) states this, explains the double-submit dance (any GET issues the token cookie; echo it via the `X-CSRF-Token` header), notes that `useAuth()` does it automatically, and shows a working curl sequence.
- **Documented where pyxle-auth accounts live.** A new [Where accounts live](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-auth.md#where-accounts-live) section covers the SQLite database auto-created at `data/app.db` on first run (no manual migration step) and how to point at a production database via pyxle-db's `url` setting; the [pyxle-db settings docs](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-db.md#plugin-settings) now also state that the file and its directory are created automatically.
- **Honest scope note on `pyxle check`.** The [CLI reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/cli.md#pyxle-check) now spells out that a green check validates syntax and semantics but does not prove a page renders ŌĆö runtime-only mistakes (e.g. a component reading a loader key that doesn't exist) surface when the page loads, not in `check`. It also notes that as of 0.7.0 the JSX level works out of the box, since pyxle-langkit ships with the framework (previously the `[langkit]` extra).
- **Prerequisites up front.** The [Introduction](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/introduction.md) now says on page one that Pyxle needs Python 3.10+ (3.12 recommended) and Node.js 20.19+ with npm, matching [Installation](https://pyxle.dev/docs/getting-started/installation.md).
- **`Accept: text/markdown` negotiation now follows RFC 9110.** The check used to be a substring match on the raw `Accept` header, which ignored q-values entirely ŌĆö `text/html, text/markdown;q=0` served Markdown even though `q=0` means *not acceptable*, and `text/html;q=0.9, text/markdown;q=0.8` served Markdown even though HTML was preferred. The header is now properly parsed: media types match by exact type/subtype token (no substrings ŌĆö `text/markdownish` no longer counts), q-values are honoured (`q=0` excludes; default `1.0`, clamped to `0..1`), wildcards like `*/*` never select Markdown, ties go to Markdown, and malformed headers never raise ŌĆö they fall back to HTML. Since negotiated responses carry `Vary: Accept`, shared caches now key on the correct variant. New public helper: `pyxle.devserver.llms.markdown_is_acceptable(accept)`. See [AI accessibility ŌåÆ Negotiation rules](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/llms.md#negotiation-rules).
- **The generated `/llms.txt` no longer advertises Markdown that isn't there.** The default index used to emit relative links and to link every concrete page's `.md` URL ŌĆö including pages whose `.md` URL just `307`-redirects to HTML. It now emits **absolute URLs** derived from the request's scheme and host (proxy headers respected), links `.md` only for pages whose Markdown actually resolves (a co-located `.md`, a `to_markdown` handler, an ancestor `llms.py` ŌĆö or every page when `autoConvert` is on), and lists the rest at their canonical HTML URL. Custom `llms_txt` hooks are unaffected; `ctx.render_default()` returns the improved index. See [AI accessibility ŌåÆ The /llms.txt index](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/llms.md#the-llmstxt-index).
- **Converted Markdown keeps agents on the Markdown channel** (breaking behavior change). `autoConvert` output ŌĆö and the public `html_to_markdown()` helper ŌĆö now rewrites internal page links to their `.md` renditions: `[About](/about)` becomes `[About](/about.md)`, preserving query strings and fragments (`/about?x=1#y` ŌåÆ `/about.md?x=1#y`). External URLs, `mailto:`/`tel:` links, `/api/` routes, asset paths with a file extension, and links already ending in `.md` are untouched, and `wrap_markdown` hooks receive the rewritten Markdown. **Breaking:** `html_to_markdown()` rewrites by default ŌĆö pass `rewrite_links=False` for the old verbatim behavior. See [AI accessibility ŌåÆ autoConvert](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/llms.md#autoconvert-the-lossy-fallback).
- **Fix: a burst of rapid file saves can no longer kill the dev server.** Two dev-server robustness fixes land together. First, build passes are now serialized and `meta.json` is written atomically and only when its content changed ŌĆö previously a debounce-timer race could run two rebuilds at once, and one pass reading the build metadata mid-write misdiagnosed it as a schema mismatch and wiped the whole `.pyxle-build` cache, deleting and recreating `vite.config.js`. Vite answers a config change with a full restart, and a restart racing the next rebuild could die (`[vite] process exited`), leaving nothing listening until a manual restart. Second, the Vite subprocess is now properly supervised: **any** exit the dev server didn't initiate ŌĆö including a "clean" exit code 0 ŌĆö logs a clear warning and relaunches Vite with a short exponential backoff (0.5s, doubling) and a bounded budget (3 consecutive attempts). If relaunching keeps failing, a structured `ViteSupervisionError` is surfaced loudly in the terminal instead of a silently dead asset server.
- **Fix: touching `request.state.<name>` without the providing plugin now explains itself.** A loader or `@action` that reads a `request.state` attribute nothing populated (most commonly `request.state.db` without the pyxle-db plugin) used to 500 with a bare `AttributeError: 'State' object has no attribute 'db'` and a raw traceback that pointed nowhere. The framework now detects exactly this case and reports it as a structured error with guidance: state attributes are provided by plugins or middleware ŌĆö e.g. `request.state.db` requires the pyxle-db plugin in `pyxle.config.json` (`"plugins": ["pyxle-db"]`). In dev the message appears in the error overlay, the action's JSON error, and the server log (with the original traceback chained); in production the response stays generic, as always. Any other `AttributeError` from your code flows through unchanged.
- **Actionable SSR error when a component touches a browser global.** A component that evaluates `window`, `document`, `navigator`, `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, `location`, `matchMedia`, or `requestAnimationFrame` at render scope used to fail SSR with a bare `window is not defined` and a traceback of framework frames ŌĆö naming the route but never your file, with no hint at the fix. Dev mode (error page, overlay, log) now explains that browser globals don't exist during server-side rendering, names the page's `.pyxl` source file, and gives the remedy: move the browser-only code into a `useEffect` hook or an event handler, or render the subtree with `<ClientOnly>` ŌĆö see the [Client Components guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/client-components.md). Production responses stay generic (no file paths or internals in the body); the enriched explanation goes to the server log. `ReferenceError`s on other names, and all other render errors, flow through exactly as before.

## 0.6.1 ŌĆö 2026-07-01

A sharper `pyxle check` ŌĆö the edit ŌåÆ check ŌåÆ fix loop now catches classes of mistake it used to wave through.

- **`pyxle check` gained a semantic layer.** Beyond Python syntax, it now runs pyflakes over the Python section and reports **undefined names** ŌĆö e.g. a handler that `raise`s a symbol it never imported ŌĆö plus unused imports and redefinitions. Compiler-injected runtime names are recognized, so the idiomatic decorators and error classes never read as undefined.
- **Duplicate `export default` is now caught.** `@babel/parser` accepts two default exports even though the build (esbuild) rejects them ŌĆö so a build-breaking mistake used to pass `check` and only fail later, pointing into a generated path. It's now flagged at check time, at the real source line.
- **JSX error lines are now accurate.** A JSX syntax error was always reported at the first line of the JSX section; it now points at the actual line. Babel's misleading "Unterminated regular expression" (its lexer's guess for an unclosed `{ }`) also carries a plain-language hint about the real cause.
- **`LoaderError` and `invalidate_routes` are now auto-injected**, matching `ActionError` / `ValidationActionError`. `raise LoaderError(...)` in a loader just works with no import ŌĆö closing an inconsistency where the action-side equivalent already did. A name you import or define yourself still takes precedence. See [Runtime API](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/runtime-api.md).
- **`pyxle install --break-system-packages`.** For externally-managed (PEP 668) Python environments without a virtualenv, `pyxle install` now offers the flag and points to it in the no-venv warning. See [CLI ŌåÆ pyxle install](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/cli.md#pyxle-install).

## 0.6.0 ŌĆö 2026-07-01

- **AI accessibility ŌĆö serve your app as markdown, plus `llms.txt`.** A new opt-in `llms` config block makes every Pyxle app legible to AI assistants and coding agents. Enable it (`"llms": true`) and the framework serves a clean **markdown** rendition of each page at its URL with `.md` appended (`/docs/routing.md`), returns markdown from the same URL to requests that send `Accept: text/markdown` (browsers are unaffected), serves an **`/llms.txt`** index, and advertises it with `Link`/`X-Llms-Txt` discovery headers. A page's markdown is resolved from your project ŌĆö a co-located `<page>.md` file, a `to_markdown` handler in the page's server module, or a `to_markdown` in the nearest ancestor `llms.py` (which scopes to a whole route subtree; `pages/llms.py` is app-wide) ŌĆö with an opt-in `autoConvert` HTMLŌåÆmarkdown fallback and a redirect-to-the-page fallback when nothing resolves. `/llms.txt` comes from a static `public/llms.txt`, a `llms_txt` hook in `pages/llms.py`, or a generated index, and a `wrap_markdown` hook in `pages/llms.py` can frame every `.md` response with a header/footer (e.g. agent navigation hints). Off by default; adds nothing to the page hot path. See the [AI accessibility guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/llms.md) and [Configuration ŌåÆ AI accessibility](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#ai-accessibility).

## 0.5.0 ŌĆö 2026-06-18

The depth release: caching/SSG/ISR, streaming SSR, realtime (WebSockets + a
cross-worker Redis broker), Pydantic-validated actions, observability,
background work, image optimization, and multi-worker serving ŌĆö built,
documented, and dogfooded on pyxle.dev. Still `0.x` (beta); the two behavior
changes below are called out explicitly.

- **Cross-worker realtime ŌĆö a Redis pub/sub broker.** WebSocket channels (`pyxle.realtime.channel` / `room.publish`) now span worker processes and machines. The default `InProcessBroker` lives in one process, so under `pyxle serve --workers N` a client on one worker never saw messages published on another. Set `PYXLE_REALTIME_BROKER=redis` (with the `pyxle-framework[redis]` extra and `PYXLE_REALTIME_REDIS_URL`) and every worker relays through Redis: a message published on any worker reaches every subscriber on every worker, delivered once per connection. **No code change** ŌĆö your handler's `channel(ws, "room")` / `room.publish(...)` are identical; only the broker behind `app.state.pyxle_broker` changes. The connection is pinged at startup (a bad URL fails loudly) and the listener reconnects automatically. The in-process broker remains the default and is correct for `pyxle dev` and single-worker serve. See [WebSockets ŌåÆ Cross-worker realtime with Redis](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/websockets.md#cross-worker-realtime-with-redis).
- **Fix: streaming SSR now survives production gzip.** Production serves enable gzip, and Starlette's stock `GZipMiddleware` buffers the whole streamed response (it never flushes the compressor between chunks), so a streamed `<Suspense>` page arrived all at once instead of shell-first ŌĆö defeating streaming for every app, since browsers always send `Accept-Encoding: gzip`. Pyxle now ships a streaming-aware `StreamingGZipMiddleware` (from `pyxle.middleware`) that flushes the compressor after every chunk, so the shell's compressed bytes reach the browser immediately. Compression and streaming are both preserved under `pyxle serve`, no configuration needed. See [Streaming SSR ŌåÆ Streaming survives gzip](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md#streaming-survives-gzip-in-production).
- **Fix: dynamic pages are no longer client-nav-cached with stale data.** The client navigation cache previously held every route's loader payload for the default window (2 minutes), so navigating away from and back to a **dynamic** page (a `@server` loader with no declared cache lifetime ŌĆö e.g. a live feed) showed stale data until a hard refresh. The navigation-cache TTL now mirrors the server's real cacheability: a dynamic page (rendered `private, no-cache`) is **not** nav-cached (TTL `0`), so back/forward always refetches and a just-made mutation shows immediately. Pages opt into nav-caching via a `cache` entry; static, loader-less pages keep the 2-minute default. See [Caching ŌåÆ Client navigation cache](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching.md#client-navigation-cache) and [Navigation cache TTL](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/client-api.md#navigation-cache-ttl).
- **Fix: `pyxle build --static` pre-renders pages whose loaders use a plugin.** Static pre-rendering ran page and layout loaders without an active plugin context, so any app that touched a plugin service in a loader ŌĆö most commonly a layout loader calling `pyxle-db`'s `get_database()` ŌĆö crashed with `No active plugin context` and pre-rendered **nothing** (0 pages). The static builder now stands up the same plugin context a request sees (loads the configured plugins and runs their startup, opening DB connections) around the pre-render, then tears it down ŌĆö so plugin-backed loaders run at build time exactly as they do at request time, which is how static generation is meant to work (read your data source at build). See [Caching ŌåÆ Static pre-rendering](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching.md#static-pre-rendering-pyxle-build-static).
- **Changed (behavior): stricter validation of nested config blocks.** The `cors`, `csrf`, `observability`, and `routeMiddleware` blocks (and `styling` / the `starlette` & `vite` network blocks) now **reject unknown keys at boot** instead of silently ignoring them. A typo'd or mis-cased nested key ŌĆö e.g. `csrf.cookieSamesite` instead of `cookieSameSite` ŌĆö that booted before now fails loudly with a clear `Unknown keys in '<block>' block` error. This is a deliberate fail-loud fix: a silently-dropped key could leave a security-relevant default in place. Fix: correct the key name. See [Configuration](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md).
- **Fix: `error.pyxl` no longer leaks internal error details in production.** When a loader or SSR render raised a non-author exception, the nearest `error.pyxl` boundary received the raw exception message (and the exception class name) as props ŌĆö potentially a Node SSR stack trace or a filesystem path ŌĆö which it could render to end users. In production the boundary now gets a generic `"An unexpected error occurred."` and a sanitized `ServerError` type; author-raised `LoaderError` / `ActionError` messages still pass through (they're meant to be shown). Dev keeps the real (secret-redacted) message. See [Error Handling](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/error-handling.md).
- **Fix: `import.meta.env.PYXLE_PUBLIC_*` is now substituted during SSR.** The Vite `define` for public env vars was applied only to the client bundle, so at server render the expression was `undefined` ŌĆö any public env var rendered into the initial HTML caused a hydration mismatch / blank first paint. The SSR esbuild step now gets the same `define` (same keys, same quoting), so server and client agree. See [Environment variables ŌåÆ Client-side variables](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/environment-variables.md).
- **Fix: scaffold `.gitignore` no longer ignores `.env`.** A fresh `pyxle init` git-ignored `.env` even though the env-vars doc tells you to commit `.env` / `.env.development` / `.env.production` and ignore only the `.local` variants. The scaffold now matches the doc.
- **Fix: `<Image>` emits a lowercase `fetchpriority` attribute.** The `priority` image rendered camelCase `fetchPriority`, which React 18.3.1 rejects with an unknown-prop warning on every such image. It now emits the valid lowercase HTML attribute.
- **Fix: production gzip no longer prints `I/O operation on closed file`.** `StreamingGZipMiddleware` now closes its `GzipFile` + buffer deterministically on every path (small/already-encoded responses, and streams interrupted mid-flight), so the GC no longer finalizes an open compressor and the noisy `Exception ignored while finalizing file ŌĆ” I/O operation on closed file` stderr messages are gone.
- **Fix: the scaffolded `jsconfig.json` no longer trips TypeScript's `baseUrl` deprecation.** VS Code (TypeScript 6+) flags `baseUrl` as deprecated ŌĆö slated for removal in TS 7 ŌĆö so every fresh project opened with a warning on a file Pyxle generated. The scaffold now declares the import alias with tsconfig-relative `paths` only (`"@/*": ["./*"]`), which resolves identically without `baseUrl`; editors, Vite, the SSR runtime, and shadcn/ui all read the alias the same way as before.
- **Fix: `pyxle typecheck` works out of the box on current TypeScript.** The generated `tsconfig.json` used `moduleResolution: "node10"` and `baseUrl`, both deprecated in TypeScript 7.0, so `tsc` failed with TS5107/TS5101 on a new-enough TypeScript. It now uses `"bundler"` resolution (correct for the Vite/esbuild pipeline) with tsconfig-relative `paths` and no `baseUrl`.
- **Startup warning when a `BaseHTTPMiddleware` is paired with streaming routes.** A custom middleware subclassing Starlette's `BaseHTTPMiddleware` buffers the whole response, which breaks streaming SSR (a `<Suspense>` page would arrive all at once / raise `No response returned` on a mid-stream disconnect). Pyxle now logs a clear warning at startup naming the offending middleware class and pointing to the pure-ASGI pattern. See [Middleware](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware.md).
- **Clear compile-time error for TypeScript syntax in a client block.** A `.pyxl` client block is plain JSX ŌĆö type annotations (`const x: number`), `interface`, generics, and `as` casts aren't supported there. Previously such syntax slipped past the compiler (Babel parses it) and failed later in esbuild/Vite with a confusing message pointing into a generated `.pyxle-build` path. The compiler now detects it up front and raises a clear error pointing at your `.pyxl` source line: "TypeScript syntax (ŌĆ”) isn't supported in a .pyxl client block yet ŌĆö keep the client half plain JSX." Detection is AST-based (any TS-only node), so valid JSX ŌĆö ternaries, object literals, the `as` prop ŌĆö is never flagged. See [TypeScript](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/typescript.md).
- **New guides: TypeScript and migrating from Flask/Django.** [TypeScript](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/typescript.md) documents Pyxle's consumption-not-authoring model ŌĆö the generated `tsconfig.json` and `.d.ts` declarations for every `pyxle/client` hook/component, the `pyxle typecheck` gate, and the honest limits (no `.tsx` authoring or per-page loader types yet). [Migrating from Flask or Django](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/migration-from-flask-django.md) maps the concepts you know (routes, views, templates, forms, the ORM, middleware, config) to their Pyxle equivalents, with a frank list of what has no direct equivalent (admin, `url_for`, flash messages, a core ORM).
- **`error.pyxl` is now a client-side error boundary too.** The server already renders the nearest `error.pyxl` when a loader or the initial render fails; now that same boundary keeps working in the browser. If a component throws while re-rendering ŌĆö after a state update, during a client-side navigation, or on a hydration fault ŌĆö Pyxle catches it and renders the nearest `error.pyxl` in place, instead of React unmounting the page to a blank screen. The boundary receives the **same** `error` prop (`message`, `statusCode`, `type`) on both sides, so one `error.pyxl` renders identically wherever the fault happened. It is transparent until something throws (so hydration is byte-for-byte unchanged) and resets on the next navigation; the nearest `error.pyxl` is resolved per route (closest-ancestor walk-up) and threaded to the client like the streaming `loading.pyxl` boundary. In `pyxle dev` the error overlay still surfaces the full stack on top; in production the boundary is the user-facing fallback. This catches *render* faults ŌĆö an error in an event handler or an `await` is handled where it occurs (a `try/catch`, or an `ActionError` from `useAction`). See [Error Handling ŌåÆ Client-side errors](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/error-handling.md#client-side-errors).
- **Built-in rate limiting ŌĆö `pyxle.middleware.RateLimitMiddleware`.** A dependency-free **token-bucket** limiter, enabled from `pyxle.config.json` with no code: `{"rateLimit": {"requests": 100, "window": 60}}`. Each client (keyed by remote IP, or the first `X-Forwarded-For` hop when `trustForwardedFor` is set behind a trusted proxy) gets a bucket of `requests` tokens that refills at `requests / window` per second ŌĆö so a brief burst is allowed while the sustained rate is bounded, friendlier than a hard fixed window. Over-limit requests get `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header. `exemptPaths` skips health checks and metrics scrapes (segment-boundary matching, like `csrf.exemptPaths`). The limiter sits just inside request observability, so a throttled request still gets a correlation id and is counted, but is rejected before CSRF, static serving, or the handler do any work; the bucket store is bounded by an LRU cap so a flood of unique clients can't grow memory without bound. **Multi-worker caveat:** the store is in-memory and per-process, so under `pyxle serve --workers N` the effective global cap is `N ├Ś requests` ŌĆö for one shared limit, rate-limit at your reverse proxy. Off by default (`requests: 0`). See [Middleware ŌåÆ Rate limiting](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware.md#rate-limiting) and [Security ŌåÆ Rate limiting](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security.md#rate-limiting).
- **Route policies now apply to `@action` endpoints.** Previously, a route hook registered under `routeMiddleware` wrapped page and API routes but **not** `@action` calls ŌĆö so an auth or rate-limit policy could be silently bypassed by hitting the action endpoint directly. Actions now run through the same route-hook pipeline; register action policies under the new `routeMiddleware.actions` list. Because an action replies with JSON, an action hook should reject with a JSON `401`/`403` rather than an HTML redirect (which is why actions get their own list instead of inheriting the page's hooks). See [Middleware ŌåÆ Route-level hooks](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware.md#route-level-hooks).
- **`pyxle serve --workers 0` auto-detects the core count.** Multi-core serving (`pyxle serve --workers N`, each worker an independent process sharing one socket) already shipped; now `--workers 0` sizes the worker count to the host's CPU cores, so you don't hard-code it per machine. The [Deployment guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/deployment.md#multi-core-worker-processes) gains a consolidated **per-worker state** table (which features are per-process ŌĆö page cache, WebSocket pub/sub, metrics, background tasks ŌĆö and the shared-backend escape hatch for each), **rolling-deploy / graceful-restart** guidance (blue-green via `/readyz`, systemd, and the gunicorn-preload option), and the rationale for the share-nothing, per-worker-SSR-pool design.
- **Build optimization: responsive `<Image>`, preload hints, and `--analyze`.** `<Image>` (from `pyxle/client`) is now an optimized image on par with Next.js's for everything that doesn't need a server-side optimizer: pass a `loader` (a CDN like Cloudinary/imgix, or a build plugin) and it emits a responsive `srcset` across a device-size ladder; it also gains `fill` mode, `sizes`, `quality`, `objectFit`, and `priority` ŌåÆ `fetchpriority="high"` for the LCP image ŌĆö plus the existing blur placeholder, `fallbackSrc`, lazy-loading, and layout-shift prevention. Without a loader it stays a clean `<img>` (no fake srcset that re-downloads the full image ŌĆö resizing needs a real backend, which stays opt-in). Separately, the SSR shell now injects `<link rel="modulepreload">` for each page's entry and the chunks it imports (from the build manifest's import graph), so they download in parallel with HTML parsing; `pyxle build --analyze` prints a dependency-free JS/CSS bundle-size report (raw + gzip); and the generated Vite config carries an explicit `build` block (faster builds ŌĆö Vite's slow gzip-size reporting is off since `--analyze` covers it). JS/CSS minification, tree-shaking, and code-splitting were already on via Vite's production defaults. See [Build Optimization](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/build-optimization.md).
- **Background tasks & deferred work.** Two ways to run work off the request's critical path. Inside an `@action`, `request.state.background.add_task(fn, *args)` (a Starlette `BackgroundTasks`) runs after the response is sent ŌĆö ideal for "send the welcome email after signup"; returning `{"background": [fn, *args]}` is shorthand for a single such task. For fire-and-forget work from anywhere (loaders, actions, API routes), the new `pyxle.tasks.enqueue(fn, *args)` schedules it on a small **in-process** worker pool ŌĆö coroutine functions are awaited, plain callables run in a thread, a failing task is logged (never takes a worker down), and the queue is bounded (`TaskQueueFull` under overload). The queue starts/stops with the app. **Multi-worker caveat:** it's per-process (like the in-process WebSocket broker), so for durability or cross-worker/exactly-once semantics, hand off to a real job queue ŌĆö the guide documents Celery, ARQ, and Dramatiq integration. See [Background Tasks](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/background-tasks.md).
- **OpenTelemetry tracing (opt-in).** Emit OpenTelemetry spans for each request plus child spans for the SSR render, `@server` loaders, and `@action` calls, so a slow page surfaces as a drill-down trace. It's the heaviest integration, so it's a **separate** optional extra (`pip install "pyxle-framework[observability-otel]"`) and **fully off** by default ŌĆö with the extra absent, spans are a zero-cost no-op (one boolean check), and the exporter endpoint comes from the standard `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` env var. Sampling defaults to 5%. A base Pyxle install pulls in no OpenTelemetry packages; enabling `observability.otel` without the extra fails loudly at startup. See [Observability ŌåÆ OpenTelemetry tracing](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md#opentelemetry-tracing).
- **`pyxle dev --dashboard` ŌĆö a live terminal observability panel.** Opt into a compact panel that refreshes every few seconds with request throughput and error rate, status-class breakdown, request/SSR-render/loader/action latency, and the page-cache hit ratio. It reads the same in-process metrics (so it's free on the request path), is **dependency-free** (no `rich`), and is dev-only ŌĆö in production you'd scrape the metrics endpoint instead. See [Observability ŌåÆ Dev dashboard](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md#dev-dashboard).
- **Structured access logging.** Opt into one structured log line per request ŌĆö `method`, `path`, `status`, `duration_ms`, and the request's correlation id ŌĆö via `observability.accessLog`, in `"console"` (human-readable) or `"json"` format. The correlation id is bound into a context variable for the request's duration, so your own log calls pick it up too. Works with **no new dependency** (a stdlib JSON/console formatter); the optional `[observability]` extra adds [structlog](https://www.structlog.org/) for richer rendering. Off by default so it won't disrupt an existing log pipeline. See [Observability ŌåÆ Structured logging](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md#structured-logging).
- **Observability: request IDs, timing, metrics, and richer health probes.** Every request now gets a correlation id ŌĆö surfaced as the `X-Request-Id` response header and on `request.state.request_id`, readable from any `@server` loader or `@action` ŌĆö and its duration is measured, along with SSR-render, loader, action, and page-cache (hit/stale/miss) metrics. The request-id and timing are **on by default** (a `uuid4` and two `perf_counter` reads, no I/O, added at the outermost layer so even a CSRF rejection is tagged); an incoming `X-Request-Id` is **not** trusted unless you opt in (`observability.trustIncomingRequestId`), since echoing a client value into logs is a spoofing vector. Metrics are recorded into a bounded, allocation-cheap in-process registry and exposed ŌĆö **opt-in** ŌĆö at a Prometheus endpoint (`observability.metricsEndpoint`, optionally bearer-guarded); the page-cache hit ratio comes from the existing server cache. `/readyz` now runs **dependency checks** (today: the SSR worker pool has a live worker) and returns `503` until the server is genuinely ready, while `/healthz` stays a pure liveness probe. All configured under the new [`observability`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#observability) block (`"observability": false` disables request-id/timing); the helper `from pyxle.observability import get_request_id` reads the id anywhere. **Multi-worker note:** metrics are per-worker (each series carries a `worker` label) ŌĆö aggregate at the scraper. Structured logging, a `pyxle dev` dashboard, and OpenTelemetry land next in Phase 2.7. See [Observability](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md).
- **Typed request validation for `@action` with Pydantic.** An action can now declare a body parameter annotated with a [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) model ŌĆö `async def register(request, body: Signup)` ŌĆö and Pyxle parses, coerces, and validates the request body before the action runs, passing in a fully-typed instance. No more hand-written `await request.json()` + key-by-key checks. On failure the action is **not** called and the client gets a `422` with a top-level `fields` map (field path ŌåÆ messages, dotted for nested models and indexed for list items: `address.zip`, `tags.0`). Both `useAction` and `<Form>` surface it ŌĆö `result.fields` / the reactive `submit.fields`, and `<Form onError={(message, fields) => ŌĆ”}>` ŌĆö so you can render messages next to each input. For checks Pydantic can't express (uniqueness, cross-field rules) raise the new `ValidationActionError`, which is auto-imported alongside `ActionError` and produces the same `422` shape. Because the body models are real Pydantic models, `pyxle openapi` generates an [OpenAPI 3.1](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0) document of every action's request body straight from your code (`--out openapi.json`), derived from runtime introspection so it always matches what the dispatcher validates. Pydantic is an **optional dependency** ŌĆö install the extra with `pip install "pyxle-framework[pydantic]"`; actions without a model parameter are unaffected, and an action that declares one without Pydantic installed fails with a clear error rather than silently skipping validation. The generated `useAction`/`Form` runtimes now also ship TypeScript declarations (`.d.ts`). See [Server Actions ŌåÆ Validating request bodies](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/server-actions.md#validating-request-bodies-with-pydantic).
- **Fix: a server module that fails to import is no longer cached as a broken empty module.** If a page's compiled server module raised during its first import (e.g. a bad top-level `import`), the half-initialised module was left in `sys.modules`, so every later request for that page silently got an *empty* module instead of re-raising the real error. Failed imports are now evicted, so the error surfaces consistently on each request.
- **WebSockets ŌĆö page handlers, a client hook, and pub/sub.** A page `.pyxl` can now export a module-scope `async def websocket(ws)` and Pyxle serves a WebSocket route at the page's path, alongside its HTTP render ŌĆö the same file handles both protocols, and dynamic segments (`pages/chat/[room].pyxl`) resolve into `ws.path_params`. Detection is by convention (the function name, not a decorator), matching how `pages/api/**` modules already expose a `websocket` callable. The new `pyxle.realtime` module ships a small pub/sub layer: `async with channel(ws, "room") as room: await room.publish(msg)` subscribes a connection to a named channel and broadcasts to everyone in it, with stale-connection pruning. Because Starlette runs HTTP middleware only for `http` scope, a WS handler gets **no** middleware ŌĆö so `pyxle.realtime` also provides `authenticate_websocket(ws)` (resolve the pyxle-auth session from the handshake cookie) and `origin_allowed(ws, origins)` (the WS equivalent of CSRF). On the client, the generated `useWebSocket(path, options?)` hook gives `{ status, send, lastMessage, error }` with JSON parsing and exponential-backoff reconnect, and never opens a socket during SSR. **Multi-worker caveat (called out honestly):** the default in-process broker lives in one process, so under `pyxle serve --workers N` a message published on one worker isn't seen by clients on another ŌĆö Pyxle logs a warning when it detects this, and the `Broker` protocol lets a Redis-backed broker drop in. See [WebSockets](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/websockets.md).
- **Streaming SSR for `<Suspense>` pages.** A page that wraps a slow part in a React 18 `<Suspense>` boundary now streams: Pyxle flushes the shell (everything outside the boundary) immediately for a fast time-to-first-byte, then streams each boundary's content in as it resolves. It's opt-in and zero-config ŌĆö the compiler detects `<Suspense>` and only those pages stream; every other page keeps the buffered render byte-for-byte. The page hydrates exactly as before (the browser hydrates the same component), so no app changes are needed beyond adding the boundary. Streaming applies to dynamic, per-request pages only: a publicly cacheable route (`{"data", "revalidate"}` envelope, `CACHE` directive, or edge `cache` entry) still renders buffered so it can be stored and `ETag`'d, and a streamed response is always `private, no-cache`. The document head is flushed before the component renders, so a streamed page uses its static `HEAD` (a runtime-rendered `<Head>` lands too late ŌĆö buffered pages are unaffected). A render error before the first byte falls back to the nearest `error.pyxl` rather than emitting a partial document. Built on the Node worker's new multi-frame `renderToPipeableStream` transport. See [Streaming SSR](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md).
- **`loading.pyxl` route-level loading states.** A `loading.pyxl` file declares a loading component that wraps the whole route in `<Suspense fallback={<Loading/>}>` ŌĆö streamed as the shell on the server and applied identically on the client so hydration matches. The nearest `loading.pyxl` wins (directory walk-up, like `error.pyxl`), and it composes with a page's own inner `<Suspense>`. Honest caveat: because the `@server` loader runs before render, the fallback only appears when the *render* suspends (a child using `React.lazy` or a thrown promise; Pyxle ships React 18.3.1, which has no `React.use`) ŌĆö a page whose data comes entirely from its loader never suspends and streams at once. See [Streaming SSR ŌåÆ Route-level loading states](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md#route-level-loading-states-with-loadingpyxl).
- **Server-side page caching with incremental regeneration.** A `@server` loader can now return a `{"data": ..., "revalidate": N}` envelope to cache its rendered HTML for `N` seconds ŌĆö later requests are served from the cache without re-running the loader or the Node SSR render. A loader-less page can opt in the same way with a module-level `CACHE = {"revalidate": N}` directive. And `pyxle build --static` pre-renders every loader-less, non-dynamic page to HTML at build time and warms the cache from it on startup, so the first request after a deploy is a hit ŌĆö no cold render. Once the window passes, the stale page is served instantly while a single background re-render refreshes it (incremental static regeneration ŌĆö no thundering herd). Purge a route on demand from an action with `await cache.invalidate("/path")` (or `cache.invalidate_all()`). Routes already declared in the `pyxle.config.json` edge `cache` block are server-cached automatically under the same TTL. Cached responses carry a strong `ETag` (so conditional requests get a `304`) and an `x-pyxle-cache: HIT|STALE|MISS` header. The cache is enabled for `pyxle serve` (off in `pyxle dev`) and stores to a bounded in-memory backend by default, or to local disk or a shared Redis via `PYXLE_PAGE_CACHE_BACKEND` (Redis needs `pip install 'pyxle-framework[redis]'`). Only cache pages that render no per-user data ŌĆö a cached render is shared with every visitor. See [Caching](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching.md).

## 0.4.5

- **Signed cookies & tokens ŌĆö `sign_cookie` / `verify_cookie`.** New stdlib-only helpers (importable from `pyxle`) attach a tamper-proof HMAC-SHA256 signature to any string ŌĆö a session id, a "remember me" token, a stateless unsubscribe or password-reset link ŌĆö and verify it later in constant time. The secret comes from `PYXLE_SECRET_KEY` (or an explicit `secret_key=`), and a `salt=` namespaces signatures so a token minted for one purpose can't be replayed for another. Signing without a secret raises `MissingSecretKeyError` rather than returning an unprotected value ŌĆö it fails closed. See [Security ŌåÆ Signed cookies and tokens](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security.md#signed-cookies-and-tokens).
- **Fix: `PYXLE_PUBLIC_*` client env vars now work in `pyxle dev` and no longer break `pyxle build`.** The generated Vite `define` emitted each value as a bare expression, so a non-identifier value (an API URL, a `0xŌĆ”` Turnstile key) was silently dropped in dev and crashed `vite build` with esbuild's "Invalid define value". Values are now emitted as quoted JS string literals, so `import.meta.env.PYXLE_PUBLIC_*` resolves correctly in both dev and production builds. See [Environment variables ŌåÆ Client-side variables](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/environment-variables.md).
- **Fix: web fonts and other CSS `url()` assets no longer 404 in `pyxle dev`.** Vite rewrites `@font-face`/`url(...)` references to root-relative `/@fs/ŌĆ”` paths, which the browser resolved against Pyxle's origin instead of Vite's and 404'd ŌĆö so `@fontsource` `.woff2` files silently failed to load in development (production builds were unaffected, as their assets are hashed and served by Pyxle). Pyxle now sets Vite's `server.origin`, so dev assets load from Vite directly.

## 0.4.4

- **Fix: cross-page hash links scroll to their anchor.** Client-side navigation to `/page#section` changed the page but stayed pinned to the top (only the URL carried the hash); a full load scrolled correctly. The router now jumps to the top and then scrolls to the anchor as soon as the next page's DOM commits, matching native behaviour ŌĆö including for unknown anchors, which stay at the top.

## 0.4.3

- **Security: hardened HEAD sanitisation (XSS).** Dynamic `HEAD` values/callables that interpolate loader data into raw head strings ŌĆö the documented dynamic-meta-tags recipe ŌĆö could previously inject markup because attribute values weren't escaped (a `"` in a `<meta content>` broke out of the attribute). Head elements are now parsed and rebuilt from a strict tag allowlist with every attribute value HTML-escaped, `on*` handlers dropped, `javascript:`/`vbscript:`/`data:` URLs neutralised, `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` and non-head tags rejected, and any markup injected after a breakout discarded. Inline `<script>`/`<style>` head content remains supported as trusted author code. Self-closing head tags now serialise canonically as `/>`.
- **Security: CSRF exempt paths match on segment boundaries.** `csrf.exemptPaths` previously used a bare string prefix, so exempting `/api/webhooks` also exempted an adjacently-named sibling like `/api/webhooks-admin`. Exemptions now match a path exactly or at a `/` boundary.
- **Security: oversized no-JS form POSTs fail loud.** A form-encoded POST whose body exceeded the CSRF buffer cap (1 MB) was silently truncated and replayed to your handler. Such requests now return `413` and ask for the token via the `X-CSRF-Token` header instead of corrupting the payload.
- **Fix: `<Script>`/`<Image>` boolean attributes written as strings.** `defer="false"`, `async="no"`, `priority="0"`, `lazy="false"` and similar quoted-falsy forms were coerced to `True` (the opposite of the source). They now coerce correctly.
- **Fix: custom `csrf.cookieName` / `csrf.headerName` now reach the client runtime.** Configuring either name made every action POST fail with `403` ŌĆö the middleware set and validated the cookie under the configured name while `useAction`/`<Form>` kept reading a literal `pyxle-csrf` cookie and sending a literal `x-csrf-token` header. Non-default names are now injected into the document shell as `window.__PYXLE_CSRF_COOKIE__` / `window.__PYXLE_CSRF_HEADER__` and the client runtime resolves them with the defaults as fallback, in both dev and production builds.
- **Fix: no double loader run on hover-then-click navigation.** When a `<Link>` was prefetched on hover/viewport and then clicked, the click raced a second request and ran the page's `@server` loader twice. The click now reuses the in-flight prefetch, and a navigation-sequence token discards a superseded prefetch so a slow one can't render a stale page over a newer click.
- **Multi-core serving: `pyxle serve --workers N`.** Production serving can now run N independent server processes on one port (each with its own SSR pool and event loop) ŌĆö throughput scales with cores, no load balancer or shared state to configure. See [Deployment ŌåÆ Multi-core (worker processes)](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/deployment.md#multi-core-worker-processes).
- **Sync API endpoints.** A plain `def endpoint(request)` in `pages/api/` now runs in Starlette's threadpool instead of failing at request time ŌĆö blocking database drivers and sync SDKs no longer require manual `asyncio.to_thread` wrapping. Sync `get`/`post`/ŌĆ” methods on `HTTPEndpoint` classes are threadpooled too, and `HTTPEndpoint` classes dispatch correctly alongside route hooks. See [API Routes ŌåÆ Sync endpoints and blocking calls](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/api-routes.md#sync-endpoints-and-blocking-calls).
- **In-memory static asset cache.** When serving a production build, small static files (Ōēż1 MB each, 32 MB total per process) are loaded into memory at startup and served without filesystem I/O or threadpool hops ŌĆö several-fold higher static throughput. Conditional requests (`ETag`/`If-Modified-Since` ŌåÆ 304) and cache headers behave exactly as before; larger files keep streaming from disk.

## 0.4.2

- **Live dev-server reconciliation.** Editing a `.pyxl` in `pyxle dev` now applies route-*shape* changes without a restart ŌĆö rename/add/remove a `@server` loader or `@action`, add or delete a page, wrap a page in a layout, change the head. The route table is rebuilt and hot-swapped on every change (these previously needed `rm -rf .pyxle-build` + a restart). Editing `pyxle.config.json` prints a clear "restart to apply" warning instead of being silently ignored.
- **`pyxle check` works on a clean install.** The JSX checker's parser dependencies are bundled into a self-contained extractor (shipped via `pyxle-langkit`), so `pyxle check` runs after `pip install 'pyxle-framework[langkit]'` with no extra npm setup. A missing-dependency failure now names the exact packages instead of an opaque error.
- **Locale-independent SSR.** The PythonŌåöNode SSR transport pins UTF-8, so emoji and other non-BMP characters in components or loader data no longer crash rendering under a non-UTF-8 locale (e.g. `LANG=C`).
- **Smoother first run.** `pyxle init` generates a gitignored `.env.local` with a random dev `PYXLE_SECRET_KEY` (CSRF HMAC on out of the box); the scaffold `requirements.txt` now declares `pyxle-framework` itself; and `pyxle install` warns about PEP 668 externally-managed environments with venv guidance instead of letting pip throw a raw wall.
- **Docs:** documented calling an `@action` endpoint directly for scripts and tests.

## 0.4.1

- **No more double loader run on first load.** The page you land on is now seeded into the client navigation cache from the server render, so the active nav link's prefetch (a `<Link>` pointing at the current route) resolves from cache instead of re-fetching. The loader runs **once**, not twice ŌĆö halving origin work on cold loads and stopping non-idempotent loaders (view counters, analytics) from firing twice. Back/forward navigation to the page is instant for the same reason.
- **Per-route navigation-cache TTL.** A route's [`cache`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#edge-caching) TTL now also governs how long its prefetched/seeded data stays fresh in the client navigation cache, so client freshness matches how long a CDN would serve the page. Routes without a `cache` entry use a default of **2 minutes** (raised from 30s), tunable via the new [`navigation.defaultPrefetchTtl`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#navigation) option.

## 0.4.0

- **Edge caching.** Declare cacheable routes in [`pyxle.config.json::cache`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#edge-caching) and pages are served `Cache-Control: public, s-maxage=N` (plus `stale-while-revalidate`) so a CDN or reverse proxy can absorb traffic instead of your origin ŌĆö Pyxle's config-driven take on per-route revalidation. The per-user CSRF cookie is automatically omitted from cacheable responses. See [Deployment ŌåÆ CDN and edge caching](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/deployment.md#cdn-and-edge-caching).
- **Hardened production errors.** SSR render failures ŌĆö including the SPA-navigation JSON path ŌĆö are sanitized in the response (no exception type, message, or path leaks to the client) while the full error is written to the server log for operators.
- **Faster static serving.** The static-asset middleware indexes public/client paths up front, skipping a per-request filesystem `stat` + exception on every dynamic request.
- **Layout & template loaders.** A `layout.pyxl` or `template.pyxl` can declare its own `@server` loader; its result lands on the component's `data` prop, just like a page ŌĆö so shared UI (nav bars, the signed-in user, the framework version) loads once per request without repeating the loader in every page. See [Layouts ŌåÆ Layout data loaders](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/layouts.md#layout-data-loaders).

## 0.3.0

- **First-class plugin system.** Compose apps via `pyxle.config.json::plugins` (Django-style `INSTALLED_APPS`) ŌĆö see the [Plugins guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/plugins.md) and [Plugins API reference](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/plugins-api.md).
- **Django-style service access.** Resolve any plugin-registered service with `from pyxle.plugins import plugin` and `plugin("auth.service")`, or use a typed shortcut shipped by the plugin (e.g. `from pyxle_auth import get_auth_service`).
- **First-party plugins.** Two official plugins land: [`pyxle-db`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-db.md) (SQLite-first with migrations) and [`pyxle-auth`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/plugins/pyxle-auth.md) (email+password sessions, argon2id, rate limits).
- **WebSocket endpoints** ŌĆö `pages/api/*.py` can export `async def websocket(ws)` for live updates, chat, log streaming. See the [API Routes guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/api-routes.md#websocket-endpoints).
- **Client navigation cache with TTL + invalidation.** Loader payloads are cached for 30s by default (tunable) so back/forward navigation is instant. Call [`invalidate(url)`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/client-api.md#invalidateurl) from the client or return [`invalidate_routes(response, ...)`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/runtime-api.md#invalidate_routesresponse-urls) from an `@action` to keep list views fresh after mutations ŌĆö automatically honoured by `useAction` and `<Form>`.
- **`ActionError` is auto-imported** for any `.pyxl` with an `@action`. No more `NameError: name 'ActionError' is not defined` on first try.
- **`<Head>` coerces multi-part `<title>` children** into a single string, silencing React's "title element received an array" warning for the common `<title>{name} ŌĆö Brand</title>` pattern.
- **SSR worker pins `LANG=en-US.UTF-8`** by default (override with `PYXLE_SSR_LOCALE`) so `toLocaleString()` and other Intl calls stop causing hydration mismatches.
- **Vite resolver prefers pinned versions.** `pyxle build` now runs `npm install` before falling back to `npx --yes vite`, so builds honour your `package.json` pin instead of fetching `vite@latest`.

---
